title: Association of cadmium with renal cancer
abstract:
ASSOCIATION OF CADMIUM WITH 
RENAL CANCER 
L,AUKENCE N. KOLONEL, MD, PHD 
Sixty-four cases of renal cancer in white males were compared with controls 
(197 cases of nonmalignant diseases of the digestive system and 72 cases of 
colon cancer) for past exposures to cadmium. Controls were also white males, 
and were group-matched to the cases on age for the analyses. Data on the 
three main sources of exposure to cadmium-diet, 
cigarette smoking and oc- 
cupation-were 
obtained by interview. T h e  results showed a significant as- 
sociation of renal cancer with exposure to cadmium, and favored a synergistic 
effect between occupational exposure and smoking. The relative risk for men 
who both smoked and worked in high-risk occupations was more than four 
times that for men who did neither. T h e  apparent synergism, however, s u g  
gests that the agent in cigarette smoke contributing to this association may 
not be cadmium. 
Cancer 37:1782-1787, 1976. 
NUMIIER OF STUDIES ASSOCIATING CADMIUM 
A with chronic diseases have appeared in 
recent years. Among these have been a few 
reports relating exposure to cadmium with 
the occurrence of malignant disease, partic- 
ularly of the lung and prostate.l.17J3 
Although cadmium has no known function 
in the human body, it is widely distributed in 
human tissues. Exposure to the element occiirs 
from a variety of sources, by both inhalation 
and ingestion. It is a constituent of most 
foods,zG cigarette tobaccos,24 and air pollu- 
tion.' In addition, occupational exposure to 
cadmium can occur in a number of industries. 
Cadmium acts as a competitive inhibitor of 
z i n ~ , ~ . l ~  
and produces malignant tumors in 
animals.lO~13 Thus, partial or total inhibition 
of regulatory, zinc-dependent enzymes is one 
possible mechanism for the carcinogenicity of 
From the Program in Epidemiology, School of Public 
Health, University of California, Barkeley, and the De- 
partments of Biostatistics and Epidemiology, Roswell 
Park Memorial Institute, Buffalo, New York. 
Supported in part by Grant No. 1-784590-33896 from 
the U.S. Public Health Service and by Grant No. 5- 
R01-HL-14783-02 from the National Cancer Institute. 
Address for reprints: Dr. L. Kolonel, Cancer Center 
of Hawaii, Epidemiology ancl Demography Unit, Uni- 
versity of Hawaii, Biomedical Sciences D-102, Honolulu, 
Hawaii 96822. 
The author thanks Dr. Warren Winkelstein, Jr., Dr. 
Irwin D. J. Bross, and Dr. Carl Keller for their many 
helpful suggestions, and Mr. John Tidings for his as- 
sistance with thc collection of the data. 
Received for publication May 16, 1975. 
the metal. Since zinc is found in significant 
quantity in the kidney, which also concen- 
trates cadmium, this organ would be a po- 
tential target for any untoward effects from 
excessive cadmium exposure. 
In the present investigation, patients with 
renal cancer and controls were examined for 
possible differences in their exposures to cad- 
mium. 
MATERIALS 
AND METHODS 
Selection of Cases and Controls 
Cases and controls were selected from pa- 
tients admitted to Roswell Park Memorial 
Institute, Buffalo, New York, from 1957 to 
1964. During these years, extensive question- 
naires were administered by interviewers to 
all admissions to the hospital. The cases in- 
cluded all white males, ages 50-79, who were 
admitted during the study period and whose 
final diagnosis was a malignant renal tumor. 
Two control groups were chosen, and were 
similarly limited to white males, ages 50-79, 
admitted during the study period. 
The first control group consisted of all pa- 
tients with a final diagnosis of a nonmalignant 
disease of the digestive system. T h e  second 
control group was comprised of cases with 
malignant tumors of the colon. A cancer con- 
trol group was included to address the prob- 
lem of potential noncomparability between 
1782 
 No. 4 
CADMIUM 
AND RENAL CANCER * 
Kolonel 
1783 
cases and controls when a noncancer control 
group is used. Due to limitations of time and 
personnel, however, not all colon cancer pa- 
tients admitted during the specified time 
period of the study could be included. Ac- 
cordingly, the study sample was restricted to 
the last 85 consecuiive cases who met the age, 
sex, and race requirements. 
Exposure Data 
Exposiires to cadmium were determined 
from the occupational, smoking, and dietary 
portions of the patient interviews. For all 
adult jobs, the occupational histories included 
the type of job, the name of the industry, and 
the number of years spent on the job. Each 
job was coded by both occupational and in- 
dustrial classification schemes,*9+30 from which 
a comprehensive list of high-risk industries 
and occupations for exposure to cadmium was 
selected (e.g. electroplating, alloy-making, 
welding, manufacture of storage batteries).*-z8 
A person was considered to have experienced 
potential occupational exposure to cadmium 
only if he had worked for 1 or more years at 
a high-risk job within a high-risk industry. 
This use of both the industrial setting and 
the specific job was an attempt to identify 
better those individuals with highly probable 
occupational exposure to cadmium. 
The detailed smoking histories permitted 
computation of each patient’s lifetime ciga- 
rette use (pack-years), which is directly related 
to cadmium intake from this source. A history 
of a t  least 10 pack-years of cigarette use during 
a lifetime was taken to represent significant 
exposure to cadmium from this source. 
Determining dietary exposure was more dif- 
ficult, since cadmium is so widely distributed 
in common food items. It was necessary, there- 
fort,, to quantify the cadmium intake through 
die!, and to select a level of intake above 
which the exposure could be considered to be 
significant. Although standard nutritional 
tables for the cadmium content of foodstuffs 
are not available, there have been a number 
of reports on the cadmium content of most 
common food i t e m ~ . 7 ~ ~ * ~ ~ ~  
By applying these 
data to the individual dietary histories (based 
on frequency recall for a I-week period), mean 
daily cadmiurn intakes were computed, assum- 
ing average size portions. An individual was 
considered “exposed” through diet if his mean 
daily intake exceeded the third quartile, de- 
termined from the distribution of intakes for 
the noncancer control group. 
RESULTS 
Completed records on all three sources of 
exposure were obtained from 64 patients with 
renal malignancies, 72 with colon cancer, and 
197 with nonmalignant gastrointestinal dis- 
eases. While the number of renal cancer cases 
is not remarkably large, this is a rare adult 
tumor and a sample of even this size is un- 
usual. Incomplete records for all three groups 
generally occurred when a patient was too 
sick to tolerate the lengthy interview. 
The results of the study are presented in 
age-specific groups, with overall weighted 
averages based on the number of cases in each 
age category. Where not otherwise noted, dif- 
ferences between cases and controls were 
tested by combining the data in the three age 
groups according to the method of Cochran.4 
Single Exposures 
The data on occupational exposures to cad- 
mium are presented in Table 1. A total of 11 
of the 64 renal cancer cases, 5 of the 72 colon 
cancer controls, and 15 of the 197 noncancer 
controls gave histories suggestive of potential 
exposure to cadmium fumes. For the renal 
cancer cases, the rate for the 60-69-year age 
category is lower than expected from the rates 
for the other two age categories. Similarly, 
for the colon cancer controls, the rate for the 
60-69-year-old category is unexpectedly high. 
Nevertheless, the generally higher exposure 
rates for the renal tumor patients are statisti-

and occupations for exposure to cadmium was

only if he had worked for 1 or more years at

rette use (pack-years), which is directly related

Completed records on all three sources of
exposure were obtained from 64 patients with
renal malignancies, 72 with colon cancer, and
197 with nonmalignant gastrointestinal dis-
eases. While the number of renal cancer cases
is not remarkably large, this is a rare adult
tumor and a sample of even this size is un-
usual. Incomplete records for all three groups
generally occurred when a patient was too
sick to tolerate the lengthy interview.
The results of the study are presented in
age-specific groups, with overall weighted
averages based on the number of cases in each
age category. Where not otherwise noted, dif-
ferences between cases and controls were
tested by combining the data in the three age
groups according to the method of Cochran.4
The data on occupational exposures to cad-
mium are presented in Table 1. A total of 11
of the 64 renal cancer cases, 5 of the 72 colon
cancer controls, and 15 of the 197 noncancer
controls gave histories suggestive of potential
exposure to cadmium fumes. For the renal
cancer cases, the rate for the 60-69-year age
category is lower than expected from the rates
for the other two age categories. Similarly,
for the colon cancer controls, the rate for the
60-69-year-old category is unexpectedly high.
Nevertheless, the generally higher exposure
rates for the renal tumor patients are statisti-
1. Occupational Exposure to Cadmium by Age Category
2. Exposure to Cadmium from Cigarette
cally significant when compared with either
control group (p < .05 in both cases).
Table 2 shows for each illness group the
proportion of smokers with significant expos-
ure (defined above) and their respective life-
time cigarette use. The exposure rates for
smoking do not differ overall among the three
disease groups, although for the two younger
age categories there are more smokers in the
renal cancer group. There are also no differ-
ences among the three disease groups in their
lifetime use of cigarettes (two-way analysis of
variance), although once again there is greater
use in the two younger age categories of the
Exposure rates for diet, using the criterion
of intakes exceeding those of 75% of the non-
cancer controls, are shown in Table 3 which
also includes the computed mean daily cad-
mium intake values for the three groups. The
proportion of renal cancer cases with unusual
dietary cadmium intake is higher than the
corresponding control values for both younger
age categories, and the mean intake values for
the renal cancer cases are at all times higher
than corresponding control values. However,
these differences between cases and controls
are not statistically significant.
The effects of cadmium exposure from more
than one source would be expected to be
additive. Indeed, levels of exposure from
single sources, with the possible exception of
certain industrial settings, would seldom be
likely to provide a sufficient dose of the metal
to result in the production of disease. Hence,
combinations of exposures from the three
sources (diet, smoking, occupation) were also
considered. Table 4 shows the exposure rates
for the cases and controls when any two or
more sources are present. The differences be-
tween the renal cancer group and both control
groups are more pronounced here, and are
consistently greater for the renal cancer cases
in all three age categories. These differences
between the cases and the controls are statis-
tically significant for the noncancer controls
(p < .05) but not for the colon cancer con-
Dietary exposure is unlike the other two
sources, not only because it is to a degree a
natural exposure, but also because it occurs
by means of the gastrointestinal tract, whereas
smoking and occupational exposures occur
primarily by the respiratory route. It is con-
ceivable that only inhaled cadmium can lead
to the occurrence of malignant disease. Hence,
a combination of the two “respiratory” ex-
posures was considered; these data are pre-
sented in Table 5. Once again, the exposure
rates for the renal cancer group are signifi-
cantly greater than those for both control
Percent Exposed to .4ny Two or More
groups (p < .01 in both instances). However,
the general pattern of higher rates for the
renal cancer group is not apparent in the
The relative risk of renal cancer was com-
puted from the age-specific data in Tables
1-5 by the method of Mantel and Haenszel.21
As seen in Table 6, for single exposure sources
the relative risk for occupational exposure is
greatest, while the highest relative risk is seen
when smoking and occupational exposure are
considered together. It is also notable that the
relative risks are quite comparable for the two
The marked increase in relative risk for
smoking and occupation together suggests the
possibility of a synergistic effect between the
two exposures. The data in Table 7 show the
relative risks for the various combinations of
these two exposures, based on a relative risk of
1.0 for the subjects with neither exposure.
The excess risk5 for the men with both ex-
posures greatly exceeds the sum of the excess
risks for each exposure considered singly, in-
dicating interaction between the two.
A number of potential biases can enter a
study of this kind. One of these is an inter-
viewer bias between cases and controls. In this
study, however, all interviews were conducted
at the time of admission to the hospital, when
both the cases and controls were thought
equally to have malignant tumors. A socio-
economic disparity between cases and controls
could also contribute to a spurious association.
The besi. indicator of socioeconomic status
available to this study was the median value
of the real estate in the respective census
tracts of residence of the case group and con-
trols. Comparison on this measure showed the
three groups to be equivalent. It might also
be noted that even the noncancer controls
were selected from a group of patients ad-
mitted, like the cases, with a tentative diag-
nosis of malignant tumor. Thus, they very
likely shared with the cases a number of un-
defined characteristics of suspected cancer pa-
tients, beyond the matched variables of age,
Throughout the analyses, similar results
were obtained for comparison of the renal
cancer cases with both control groups. This
consistency between controls strengthens these
findings, of course. However, that the asso-
5 .  Percent with Respiratory Exposure (Both
Smoking and Occupation) to Cadmium
ciation is seen with the cancer control group
implies some specificity for the relationship;
that is, all cancer patients do not show this
association with increased cadmium exposure,
a possible explanation if only the noncancer
When the exposure sources are considered
separately, occupational exposure shows the
highest relative risk and is the only source
which alone yielded a statistically significant
association with renal cancer. This finding is
not unexpected, of course, since industrial
settings provide the heaviest exposure levels.
The fact that smoking and occupation to-
gether produce the greatest relative risk,
greater than diet and smoking or diet and
occupation, suggests that indeed the route of
exposure, perhaps even more than the degree,
may be of major significance in this type of
disease process. Teleologically, this would cer-
tainly be reasonable, since the respiratory ex-
posures are artificial, while exposure through
diet is basically natural (though man may
have increased it to an unnatural level in this
The apparent synergism between smoking
and occupation is consistent with the findings
of other studies.19,27 Synergism implies that
two different factors are interacting in the
production of a certain outcome (here dis-
ease). In this study, the original hypothesis
6. Relative Risk of Renal Cancer for the
7. Relative Risk of Renal Cancer for Occupational and/or Smoking Exposure Based on Noncancer Controls
t Negative value reflects small numbers.
was that the same factor, cadmium, was pro-
viding the adverse exposure in both the ciga-
rette smoke and in the work environment;
thus, the combined effect was expected to be
additive. The interaction effect suggests that
the factor associated with smoking (or, con-
versely, that associated with the selected risk
occupations) may not be cadmium, but rather
some other component of the exposure. Differ-
ent forms of cadmium in the two exposures
(e.g. ionic vs. metal powder) could be con-
sidered, but all cadmium is rapidly converted
to the divalent cation in the body. Since the
frequencies for certain categories of exposure
in Table 7 are not large, however, this sy-
nergism is only suggested by these data.
The evidence for cadmium as a carcinogen
in man is meager. Morgan22 found that the
cadmium content of uninvolved renal and
hepatic tissue of patients who died from bron-
chogenic carcinoma was elevated over that of
controls. The association of bronchogenic car-
cinoma with emphysema, which in turn has
been associated with chronic cadmium ex-
posure, could not adequately account for the
differences she found.23 However, cadmium is
a component of cigarette tobacco and, there-
fore, can be expected to show an association
with any disease entity which has an estab-
lished relationship to cigarette smoking.
Humperdinckll found no evidence for in-
creased lung cancer rates in a group of work-
ers in a cadmium-utilizing factory in Ger-
More striking evidence for the possible
carcinogenicity of cadmium in man has been
reported from Britain, where deaths from car-
cinoma of the prostate have been associated
with cadmium exposure in industrial workers.
In one alkaline battery factory, Potts25 ob-
served three deaths due to prostatic carcinoma
among eight deaths in workers with greater
than 10 years' exposure to cadmium oxide
dust. In a later report from the same plant,
Adams et al.1 noted that two out of four can-
cer deaths among 11 deaths in workers with
known exposure to cadmium during a 12-year
period were from prostatic carcinoma. Kipling
et a1.17 surveyed tumor occurrences in a group
of 248 workers with at least 1 year's exposure
to cadmium oxide. Based on expected cancer
frequencies in this group, the observed fre-
quency for prostatic carcinoma was signifi-
cantly elevated. No other tumor they consid-
ered showed a similarly increased frequency.
This association between prostatic cancer and
cadmium exposure has not been reported
from other countries, however,2o although
both the Nashville and the Buffalo air pol-
lution studies showed a direct relationship
between the level of suspended particulate
matter in air and death rates for carcinoma
of the pr0state.ll.3~ As a recognized com-
ponent of air pollution, cadmium affords one
possible etiologic explanation for this associa-
The present study suggests another associa-
tion of the metal with human cancer, in this
case cancer of the kidney. The plausibility
of such a relationship and a possible mecha-
nism involving zinc were suggested earlier. It
is notable that of all organs, the kidney, with
a high content of zinc, concentrates cadmium
most heavily. The kidney also contains the
body's highest concentration of sulfhydryl
groups6 which are often found in zinc-contain-
ing enzymes. Cadmium inhibition of a variety
of sulfhydryl-containing enzymes has been
An association of renal cancer with smoking
has been reported.3.12 The findings of the
present study suggest that this observation
may be explained in part by a synergistic
effect between smoking and exposure to cad-
mium, although a direct effect from the cad-
the use of cigarettes continues to increase,
mium in cigarette smoke (especially in the
despite active campaigns against smoking, and
absence of other cadmium exposure) also can-
since trace metals like cadmium are being
found in ever-increasing amount in our food,
This association of renal tumors with ex-
water, and air, studies to determine the ulti-
posure to cadmium and the apparent inter-
mate health effects from such exposure are
action with smoking merit further study. Since
